coredata.dc:title: Electrochemical detection of omethoate and acetamiprid in vegetable and fruit with high sensitivity and selectivity based on pomegranate-like gold nanoparticle and double target-induced DNA cycle signal amplification
coredata.dc:creator: Ruiyi, Li
Jin, Wang
Nana, Li
Dan, Xu
Zaijun, Li
coredata.dc:description: The study reports synthesis of gold-graphene quantum dot nanohybrid via reduction of chloroauric acid with arginine and aspartic acid-functionalized graphene quantum dot (Arg/Asp-GQD). The Arg/Asp-GQD-Au offers pomegranate-like architecture and Schottky heterojunction and was used for construction of electrochemical sensor for detection of omethoate and acetamiprid coupled with double target-induced DNA cycles. Target molecule hybridizes with aptamer DNA in duplex DNA to release auxiliary strand DNA. This triggers the DNA cycle and brings one redox probe to electrode surface. By the DNA cycle, one target molecule can transfer many redox probes to electrode surface and produces a significant signal amplification. The detection signal was enhanced by catalysis of Arg/Asp-GQD-Au toward redox of these probes due to its excellent catalytic activity. Differential pulse voltammetric peak current linearly increases with increasing target concentration between 5 × 10−14 and 5 × 10−10 M with detection limit of 1.67 × 10−14 Μ (S/N = 3) for omethoate and between 1 × 10−14 and 5 × 10−10 M with detection limit of 3.33 × 10−15 M (S/N = 3) for acetamiprid. The proposed method provides a much better sensitivity and selectivity and was successfully applied to detection of omethoate and acetamiprid in vegetable and fruit.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Gold nanoparticle
Functional graphene quantum dot
Electrochemical sensor
Omethoate
Acetamiprid

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Electrochemical detection of omethoate and acetamiprid

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM (A), enlarged SEM (B), TEM (C), high resolution TEM (HRTEM) (D), HAADF-STEM images (E) and EDS elemental mappings of gold (Au) (F), chlorine (Cl) (G), carbon (C) (H) and nitrogen elements (N) (I) of Arg/Asp-GQD-Au.
XRD pattern (A) and FTIR spectrum of the as-synthesized Arg/Asp-GQD-Au.
Total (A), C1 s (B), N1 s (C), O1 s (D) and Au4 f XPS spectra (E) of Arg/Asp-GQD-Au and Mott-Schottky plots (F) of Arg/Asp-GQD and Arg/Asp-GQD-Au.
Sensing principle for electrochemical detection of omethoate and acetamiprid.

CV curves and EIS plots in a 1.0 mM K4[Fe(CN)6] in the PBS of pH 7 of the bare GE (a), H1 +H3/GE (b), MCH/H1 +H3/GE (c) and MCH/H1 +H3/GE incubated in a H2-Au-THI and H4-Au-FC solution in the absence (d) and the presence of 10−11 M omethoate and acetamiprid (e).

DPV curves (A) in the PBS of pH 7.0 in the absence (a) and presence (b) of 5 × 10−10 M acetamiprid and DPV curves (B) in the PBS of pH 7.0 in the absence (a) and presence (b) of 5 × 10−10 M omethoate.

DPV curves (A) in the PBS of pH 7 in the presence of 5 × 10−14, 1 × 10−13, 5 × 10−13, 1 × 10−12, 2 × 10−12, 5 × 10−12, 1 × 10−11, 2 × 10−11, 5 × 10−11, 1 × 10−10 and 5 × 10−10 M omethoate and acetamiprid (from bottom to top), relationship curve (B) of FC DPV peak current (Ip(FC)) with the logarithm of omethoate, DPV curves (C) in the PBS of pH 7 in the presence of 1 × 10−14, 5 × 10−14, 1 × 10−13, 5 × 10−13, 1 × 10−12, 5 × 10−12, 1 × 10−11, 5 × 10−11, 1 × 10−10 and 5 × 10−10 M acetamiprid (from bottom to top), and relationship curve (D) of change in the DPV peak current (Ip) with the logarithm of acetamiprid concentration. In Fig. 8B and D all data are the average value of five detections that their relative standard deviations are less than 5%.

The ΔIp(FC) values (A) in the presence of different pesticides and the ΔIp(THI) (B) in the presence of different pesticides. a, b, c, d, e, f, g, h and i presnet imidacloprid, cypermethrin, fenvalerate, chlorothalonil, chlorfenapyr, fipronil, biphenthrin, difenoconazole, omethoate and acetamiprid, respectively. The concentration of omethoate and acetamiprid is 0.01 nM, the concentration of other pesticides is 1 nM. All data in the figure are the average value of five detections that their relative standard deviations are less than 3%.
Comparison with analytical methods for electrochemical detection of omethoate and acetamiprid.
The results of electrochemical detection of omethoate and acetamiprid in vegetables and fruits (N = 5).

row.entry: omethoate detected by the proposed method (pM)
acetamiprid detected by the proposed method (pM)

head.ce:title: Electrochemical detection of omethoate and acetamiprid in vegetable and fruit with high sensitivity and selectivity based on pomegranate-like gold nanoparticle and double target-induced DNA cycle signal amplification

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Synthetic and Biological Colloids, Ministry of Education, School of Chemical and Material Engineering, Jiangnan University, Wuxi 214122, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Synthetic and Biological Colloids, Ministry of Education, School of Chemical and Material Engineering, Jiangnan University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Synthetic and Biological Colloids, Ministry of Education, School of Chemical and Material Engineering, Jiangnan University, Wuxi 214122, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Pharmaceutical Science, Jiangnan University, Wuxi 214122, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Pharmaceutical Science, Jiangnan University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Pharmaceutical Science, Jiangnan University, Wuxi 214122, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The study reports synthesis of gold-graphene quantum dot nanohybrid via reduction of chloroauric acid with arginine and aspartic acid-functionalized graphene quantum dot (Arg/Asp-GQD). The Arg/Asp-GQD-Au offers pomegranate-like architecture and Schottky heterojunction and was used for construction of electrochemical sensor for detection of omethoate and acetamiprid coupled with double target-induced DNA cycles. Target molecule hybridizes with aptamer DNA in duplex DNA to release auxiliary strand DNA. This triggers the DNA cycle and brings one redox probe to electrode surface. By the DNA cycle, one target molecule can transfer many redox probes to electrode surface and produces a significant signal amplification. The detection signal was enhanced by catalysis of Arg/Asp-GQD-Au toward redox of these probes due to its excellent catalytic activity. Differential pulse voltammetric peak current linearly increases with increasing target concentration between 5 × 10−14 and 5 × 10−10 M with detection limit of 1.67 × 10−14 Μ (S/N = 3) for omethoate and between 1 × 10−14 and 5 × 10−10 M with detection limit of 3.33 × 10−15 M (S/N = 3) for acetamiprid. The proposed method provides a much better sensitivity and selectivity and was successfully applied to detection of omethoate and acetamiprid in vegetable and fruit.

ce:section.ce:para: Pesticides plays an important role in protecting agricultural production, improving agricultural comprehensive production capacity, promoting high yield and stability of grain and oil, increasing farmers' income, and so on [1]. Pesticides have become an indispensable means of production and disaster relief materials for modern agriculture [2]. However, pesticide utilization is lower than 10%. Approximately 90% of pesticide residues are in the environment, resulting in environmental pollution [3]. A large number of pesticides are lost through volatilization into the air, flowing into the water bodies, settling and accumulating in the soil, pollute agricultural produce, livestock, and fishery fruit products, and finally transfer to the human body through the food chains [4]. Investigations showed that exposure to pesticides is toxic to fetuses, children, and adults. Moreover, a low dose of pesticide may cause chronic poisoning in humans and animals, lead to diseases, and even affect the next generation [5]. Therefore, it is critical to develop highly reliable analytical methods for broad spectrum screening and monitoring pesticide residues in fresh food.

Furthermore, techniques have been developed for the detection of pesticide residues, such as gas chromatography (GC) [6], high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [7], gas chromatography–mass spectrometry (GC-MS) [8], high performance liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (HPLC–MS) [9], surface enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) [10], synthetic biology [11], fluorescence method with a luminescent material [12], visual protein chip method with smartphones [13], colorimetric method, with natural enzyme, [14] and electrochemical sensor [15]. GC-MS is the most powerful tool for the detection of pesticides in foods in the laboratory and can offer a large capacity database; thus, pesticide species corresponding to different peaks in the chromatogram can be easily obtained by the data retrieval. However, GC-MS requires complex sample pretreatment to remove biomacromolecules in foods and expensive instruments. These limit its wide applications in rapid detection and field analysis. The colorimetric method using a natural enzyme is the most commonly used method for rapid detection of pesticide residues in foods because of its simplicity, rapidity, and low cost. However, enzyme activity is susceptible to environmental temperature and acidity, inconveniencing practical application. In addition, its sensitivity is significantly lower than that of GC-MS and electrochemical sensors. Recently, electrochemical sensors received more attention due to their high sensitivity, selectivity, and rapidity [16].

Several nanomaterials have been designed and applied in the electrochemical detection of pesticides to improve the analytical performance [17]. Gold (Au) and graphene are the commonly used sensing materials for the electrochemical detection of pesticides because of their unique structures and properties. Au has special catalytic activity and high electrical conductivity, but Au nanoparticles agglomerate into large Au nanoparticles during electrochemical detection. This will lose the part of specific surface area of Au nanoparticles and leads to a reduced catalytic activity [18]. Unlike Au, graphene is a two-dimensional material with a large surface and high electrical conductivity; however, graphene has no special catalytic ability [19]. Owing to complementarity in structure and nature, a hybrid of Au and graphene should effectively enhance the electrochemical properties [20]. Research has shown that Au–graphene composites always offer a better catalytic activity than single Au and graphene [21]. However, the Au–graphene composite is highly hydrophobic. This characteristic is not conducive to hydrophilic electrolyte approaching electrode surface and forms a stable aqueous dispersion for construction of electrochemical sensor. In particular, the efforts to overcome particle agglomeration of Au nanoparticles have not achieved ideal results due to the lack of strong interaction between the Au nanoparticle and graphene sheet. In the previous study, our groups attempted to use graphene quantum dots instead of common graphene for making a Au–graphene hybrid [22]. The resultant hybrids indicate good water solubility and catalytic activity. Furthermore, the introduction of graphene quantum dot also brings special functions, such as the ability to capture tumor cells [23] and recognize target molecules to produce electrochemical signals [24]. However, these Au–graphene hybrids lack stable three-dimensional structures, which limit further improvement in analytical performance for electrochemical detection of pesticides

This study reports one strategy for the synthesis of Au nanoparticle-arginine and aspartic acid-functionalized graphene quantum dot hybrid (Arg/Asp-GQD) via simple one-step reduction of HAuCl4 with Arg/Asp-GQD. The as-synthesized Arg/Asp-GQD-Au nanoparticle hybrid (Arg/Asp-GQD-Au) shows the pomegranate-like architecture composed of small Au nanocrystals with the size of only several nanometers and Schottky heterojunctions at the interface. The unique structure achieves excellent catalytic activity. Furthermore, the electrochemical sensor based on Arg/Asp-GQD-Au and double target-induced DNA cycle provided the advantage of sensitivity, selectivity, and stability and was successfully applied in simultaneous detection of omethoate (OME) and acetamiprid (ACE) in vegetables and fruits.

Omethoate aptamer (AP-O, 5′-AAGCTTTTTTGACTGACTGCAGCGATTCTTGATCGCCACGGTCTGGAAAAAGAG-3′), auxiliary strand DNA of omethoate aptamer (AS-O, 5′-CGATCAAGAATCGCTGCAGTAAAAA-3′), acetamiprid aptamer (AP-A, 5′-TGTAATTTGTCTGCAGCGGTTCTTGATCGCTGACACCATATTATGAAGA-3′), auxiliary strand DNA of acetamiprid aptamer (AS-A, 5′-TGTCAGCGATCAAGAACCGCGGGGG-3′), hairpin DNA 1 (H1, 5′-SH-(CH2)6-CCCCCGCGGTTCTTGATCGCTGACAGG TCTGCGTGTAAAGCGATCAAGAACCGCCT-3′), hairpin DNA 2 (H2, 5′-GCGGTTCTTGATCGCTTTACACGCAGACCTGTCAGCGA TCAAGAACCGCGGGGG-(CH2)6-SH-3′), hairpin DNA 3 (H3, 5′-SH-(CH2)6-TTTTTACTGCAGCGATTCTTGATCGGGTCTGCGT GTAAAAAGAATCGCTGCAGTCT-3′), and hairpin DNA 4 (H4, 5′-ACTGCAGCGATTCTTTTTACACGCAGACCCGATCAAGAAT CGCTGCAGTAAAA-(CH2)6-SH-3′).

The Arg/Asp-GQD solution (10 mL, 5 mg mL−1) was heated to 95 °C. After added the chloroauric acid solution (1.27 mL, 10 mM), the solution was heated for 1 min at 95 °C, cooled to room temperature and centrifugated at 10,000 rpm for 10 min in sequence. The collected Arg/Asp-GQD-Au was again dispersed in 1 mL of ultrapure water to form Arg/Asp-GQD-Au dispersion. Before use, the dispersion was stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C.

H2 and H4 were heated at 95 °C for 5 min and then cooled to room temperature to form special hairpin structures, respectively. The pre-treated H4 solution (10 μM, 50 μL) was mixed with the tris(2-carboxyethyl)phosphine (TCEP) solution (1 mM, 50 μL). The mixed solution was incubated for 30 min, then added 50 μL of the Arg/Asp-GQD-Au dispersion, incubated for 12 h and centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 10 min to remove the unbound H2. To obtain H4-Arg/Asp-GQD-Au-ferrocene redox probe (termed as H4-Au-FC), the collected H4-Arg/Asp-GQD-Au was mixed with the sulfhydryl modified ferrocene (FC-SH) solution (0.1 mM, 150 μL). Followed by incubation for 12 h, centrifugation at 8000 rpm for 10 min to remove the unbound FC-SH and addition of 1.0 mL Tri-EDTA buffer (TE buffer). The above procedure unless H4 was replaced by H2 and FC-SH was replaced by thionine (THI) was also used for the preparation of H2- Arg/Asp-GQD-Au-thionine redox probe (termed as H2-Au-THI).

H1 and H3 were heated at 95 °C for 5 min and then cooled to room temperature to form special hairpin structures. The pre-treated H1 aqueous solution (10 μM, 10 μL), pre-treated H3 aqueous solution (10 μM,10 μL) and TCEP aqueous solution (1 mM, 20 μL) were mixed and subsequently incubated for 30 min at room temperature. Its 5 μL was dropped on the surface of gold electrode and then incubated for 12 h at room temperature, washed with ultrapure water and dried in N2 current in sequence. It was treated by dropping 5 μL of the mercaptohexanol (MCH) solution (1 mM) on the electrode surface to block the active sites on the gold electrode, incubation for 60 min at room temperature, washed with ultrapure water and drying at 4 °C orderly. The obtained sensor was stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C before use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical detection of omethoate and acetamiprid
ce:section.ce:para: Before the detection, the AP-O aqueous solution (10 μM) was mixed with an equal volume of AS-O (10 μM) and then incubated at 37 °C for 60 min to form AP-O/AS-O duplex DNA. The above procedure was also used for preparation of AP-A/AS-A duplex DNA, unless AP-O was replaced by AP-A and AS-O was replaced by AS-A. For the detection, 10 μL of AP-O/AS-O duplex DNA, 10 μL of AP-A/AS-A duplex DNA, 40 μL of the omethoate (OME) standard solution and 40 μL of the acetamiprid (ACE) standard solution with different concentration were mixed and then incubated at 37 °C for 30 min. The 20 μL of above mixed solution was mixed with 10 μL of the H4-Au-FC solution (1.6 μM), 10 μL of the H2-Au-THI solution (1.6 μM) and 40 μL of TE buffer. Its 5 μL was dropped onto the sensor surface, incubated 37 °C for 60 min, washed with ultrapure water and finally used for the DPV measurement in a phosphate-buffered solution (PBS) (Na2HPO4-NaH2PO4-KCl, 0.1 M, pH 7.0).

The scheme for the synthesis of Arg/Asp-GQD-Au is shown in Fig. 1. The synthesis only needs a one-step reduction of HAuCl4 with Arg/Asp-GQD. SinceArg/Asp-GQD has a strong reduction ability, the reduction can be completed within 2 min. When Arg/Asp-GQD-Au was added into the HAuCl4 solution, the color of the reaction system rapidly changed to black from yellow. In the process, Au3+ was reduced to Au0 and finally formed Au nanocrystals. Meanwhile Arg/Asp-GQDs were immobilized on the surface of Au nanocrystals by the strong interaction between Au nanocrystals and Arg/Asp-GQDs. Immobilization improves the structural stability and water solubility of Au nanocrystals. Different from the common stabilizer, Arg/Asp-GQD is a polydentate ligand. Thus, a graphene sheet in Arg/Asp-GQD can be combined with several Au nanocrystals via chemical bonds. The combination causes rapid self-assembly of different Au nanocrystals and leads to the formation of a three-dimensional structure, consisting of numerous Au nanocrystals and small graphene sheets. Unlike classical graphene, Arg/Asp-GQD is an N-type semiconductor. The combination of Au nanocrystals with Arg/Asp-GQDs creates Schottky heterojunctions at the interface, improves the catalytic activity of Arg/Asp-GQD-Au.

The as-synthesized Arg/Asp-GQD-Au was characterized by SEM, TEM, HAADF-STEM, elemental mappings, XRD, IR spectrum, XPS, and Mott–Schottky measurement. Fig. 2 depicts the SEM, enlarged SEM, TEM images, high resolution TEM (HRTEM) image, HAADF-STEM image, and EDS elemental mappings of Au, chlorine (Cl), carbon (C), and nitrogen elements (N) of Arg/Asp-GQD-Au. Arg/Asp-GQD-Au has a pomegranate-like structure with a relatively large particle size of approximately 300 nm. Each particle of Arg/Asp-GQD-Au is composed of small Au nanoparticles with a few nanometers. In small rich porous structures Arg/Asp-GQDs is a polydentate linker, connecting different Au nanoparticles into a whole. A lattice fringe of the (111) with a d-spacing of 0.23 nm can be observed on the HRTEM image. The spatial distribution of different elements in Arg/Asp-GQD-Au particles was shown in the analysis of element mappings. Since Au and Cl elements come from the HAuCl4, and C and N elements come from Arg/Asp-GQD, the abovementioned results also demonstrate the existence of Au nanocrystal and Arg/Asp-GQD in Arg/Asp-GQD-Au. The unique architecture is beneficial to improve analytical performance. The small size of Au nanoparticles in Arg/Asp-GQD-Au increases the exposure of Au atoms and brings increased atomic utilization, leading to enhanced catalytic activity. Furthermore, the existence of small pores in Arg/Asp-GQD-Au particles speeds up the electrolyte transport between the electrode and electrolyte, which accelerates the electrode reaction and results in an increased sensitivity. The strong interaction between different Au nanoparticles enhances the structural stability of Arg/Asp-GQD-Au; therefore, it helps to enhance the repeatability for electrochemical detection of OME and ACE.

The XRD pattern and IR spectrum of Arg/Asp-GQD-Au are presented in Fig. 3. On the XRD pattern, there are four diffraction peaks at 38.3°, 44.5°, 64.9°, and 77.8°, corresponding to the (111), (200), (220), and (311) facets of Au nanocrystal. However, no characteristic peak at 26° of classical graphene appears in the XRD pattern since graphene in Arg/Asp-GQD-Au offers a poor crystallinity. On the FTIR spectrum, there are six IR absorption peaks; the peak at 3429 cm−1 is caused by the symmetric vibration of N-H bond; the peak at 2946 cm−1 is due to the stretching vibration of the C-H bond; the peak at 1662 cm−1 is the IR absorption produced by stretching vibration of the CO bond; the peak at 1635 cm−1 is attributed to the bending vibration of the N-H bond plane and asymmetric stretching vibration of C-O bond; the peak at 1392 cm−1 is because of the IR absorption of -N-CO bond; and the peak at 1041 cm−1 is caused by out of plane bending vibration. These results show the existence of Arg/Asp-GQD in the Arg/Asp-GQD-Au hybrid.

Fig. 4 indicates the XPS spectra of Arg/Asp-GQD-Au and Mott–Schottky plots of Arg/Asp-GQD and Arg/Asp-GQD-Au. The total XPS spectrum includes four peaks at 86, 284, 399, and 532 eV, corresponding to the Au4f, C1s, N1s, and O1s XPS. The result reveals that Arg/Asp-GQD-Au mainly comprises Au, C, O, and N elements. The high-resolution C1s XPS spectrum has four peaks at 284.4, 284.9, 286.4, and 288.7 eV. These peaks can be assigned to the C-N, C-C, C-O, and CO bonds indicating the existence of -OH, -NH2, and -COOH groups. The high -resolution N1s XPS spectrum includes two peaks at 398.7 and 400.3 eV, corresponding to graphite N and amide N. The graphitic N comes from the N-doping at high temperature during the preparation of Arg/Asp-GQD, and amide N comes from the -N-CO and NHCO groups produced by amidation reaction in the synthesis of Arg/Asp-GQD. The high-resolution O1s XPS spectrum can be divided to 531.4 and 533.2 eV peaks. These peaks correspond to the O-C and OC bonds, verifying the existence of -OH and -COOH groups. The high-resolution Au4f XPS spectrum contains four peaks at 83.9, 84.6, 87.6, and 89.2 eV. These peaks can be assigned to the Au04 f7/2, Au+14 f5/2, Au04 f5/2, and Au+14 f7/2. The result demonstrates the existence of Au0 and Au+1 species on the surface of Au nanoparticles.

The Mott–Schottky plots of Arg/Asp-GQD and Arg/Asp-GQD-Au were measured to confirm the formation of Schottky heterojunctions. Fig. 4F shows the line section of two Mott–Schottky plots that offer a positive slope,demonstrating Arg/Asp-GQD and Arg/Asp-GQD-Au belong to N-type semiconductors. Further, the Mott–Schottky plots are used for extrapolating the flat band potential (Vb) of Arg/Asp-GQD and Arg/Asp-GQD-Au. The Vb value is − 0.24 V for Arg/Asp-GQD and − 0.56 V for Arg/Asp-GQD-Au. These data show that a hybrid of Arg/Asp-GQD and Au nanoparticle produces a more negative Vb shift, leading to an up-shift of the Fermi level of Arg/Asp-GQD. The up-shift of the Femi level is beneficial to increase the electron concentration and electrical conductivity.

A sensing platform was designed and used for electrochemical detection of ACE and OME ( Fig. 5). To fabricate an electrochemical sensor, two hairpin DNA probes, H1 and H3, are immobilized on the surface of the Au electrode (GE) by the Au-S bond. Then, MCH was dropped on the surface of the GE to block the remaining active sites. The as-prepared electrochemical sensor was applied in the detection of ACE and OME. For the detection, ACE hybridizes AP-A/AS-A duplex DNA to release one AS-A, and OME hybridizes AP-O/AS-O duplex DNA to release one AS-O. The released AP-A and AP-O trigger the DNA cycle I and the DNA cycle II, respectively, bringing THI and FC-based redox probes to the electrode surface, producing a sensitive electrochemical response by the redox of these FC molecules. Through the DNA cycle I and II, several H2-Au-THI probes were brought to the electrode surface by one ACE molecule, and H4-Au-FC probes were also brought to the electrode surface by one OME molecule. A significant signal amplification was produced, which was further enhanced by the in situ catalysis of Arg/Asp-GQD-Au toward redox of THI and FC probes due to high catalytic activity. However, DNA cycle I and II cannot be triggered in the absence of ACE and OME. Since redox probes were brought to the electrode surface during the incubation, the electrode produces a weak background electrochemical signal. From the abovementioned analysis, only the target molecule can cause a sensitive electrochemical response; thus, the as-proposed electrochemical sensor can be used to detect ACE and OME.

The cyclic voltammetry (CV) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) behaviors of different electrodes were investigated in a 1.0 mM of K4[Fe(CN)6] in the PBS of pH 7 to examine the feasibility of the proposed sensing platform, and the results are shown in Fig. 6 . CV curves indicate a pair of redox peaks, corresponding to the oxidation and reduction of Fe(CN)6 4−, on the electrode surface. H1 +H3/GE offers a smaller CV current and a bigger electron transfer impedance (Rct) than the bare GE, since the immobilization of H1 and H3 will partly block the electron transfer channels between the electrode and the electrolyte. As a result, the electron conduction resistance increases, leading to a reduced CV current and an increased Rct. Similar to the immobilization of H1 and H3, the modification of MCH further decreases the CV current and increases the Rct. In the absence of OME and ACE, MCH/H1 +H3/RE incubation in an H2-Au-THI and H4-Au-FC solution only causes a slight change in the CV current and Rct,In the absence of OME and ACE, the DNA cycle I and the DNA cycle II cannot be triggered. As no redox probes were brought to the electrode surface, the incubation cannot produce an obvious change in the CV current and Rct. However, in 10−11 M of OME and ACE, the incubation brings an increased CV current. During the incubation, OME and ACE can specifically bind with AP-O in AP-O/AS-O duplex DNA and AP-A in AP-A/AS-A duplex DNA to release one AS-O and one AS-A. The released AS-O and AS-A trigger the DNA cycle I and cycle II, respectively. As a result, one H2-Au-THI probe and one H4-Au-FC probe were brought to the electrode surface. By the DNA cycle I and cycle II, one OME molecule brings many H2-Au-THI probes to the electrode surface, and one ACE molecule brings many H4-Au-FC probes to the electrode surface. These redox probes on the electrode surface produces a sensitive electrochemical response. Thus, the proposed sensing platform can be used for electrochemical detection of OME and ACE.

We studied the electrochemical response toward OME and ACE by monitoring the differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) behavior of the proposed sensor in the PBS (pH 7) in the absence and presence of 5 × 10−10 M OME and ACE. The results are shown in Fig. 7 . The DPV curve approximates a straight line in the absence of ACE, and no DPV peak was observed in the potential range of 0–0.5 V (Fig. 7A). In the absence of ACE, the DNA cycle I cannot be triggered due to the lack of free AS-A. As no H2-Au-THI probes were brought to the electrode surface during the incubation, the sensor offers a very weak DPV current. However, in the presence of ACE, the sensor produces an enhanced DPV peak current. High DPV peak is attributed to the redox of THI-based probes on the electrode surface. In the presence of ACE, the DNA cycle I is induced. One ACE molecule can bring many H2-Au-THI probes to the electrode surface by the DNA cycle I, producing a sensitive DPV current response. Similar to electrochemical detection of ACE, in the presence of OME, an obvious DPV peak appears in the DPV curve in the potential range from − 0.5 to − 0.1 V (Fig. 7B) since OME can bind with the AP-O/AS-O duplex DNA to release one free AS-O from the duplex DNA. Moreover, the released AS-O triggers the DNA cycle II and brings one H4-Au-FX probe to the electrode surface. Furthermore, through the DNA cycle II, one OME can transfer several H4-Au-FC probes to the electrode surface, which leads to a significant signal amplification.

To optimize the reaction conditions, the effect of incubation time and probe concentration on the DPV currents caused by 1 × 10−11 M ACE or 1 × 10−11 M OME was investigated in a PBS of pH 7.0. Fig. S1 represents the relationship curves between the change in DPV peak current of H2-Au-THI probes (ΔIp(THI) and the incubation time and between the change in DPV peak current of H4-Au-FC probes (ΔIp(FC) with the incubation time. Fig. S1 shows that ΔIp(FC) and ΔIp(THI) will rapidly increase with increasing the incubation time when the incubation time is < 60 min for ACE and OME This increase is because the number of DNA cycles increases with incubation time. As a result, more H2-Au-THI and H4-Au-FC probes were immobilized on the electrode surface during the incubation. Moreover, the redox of these probes produces an increased DPV current. However, ΔIp(FC) and ΔIp(THI) values are up to maximum when the incubation time reaches 60 min and then remain stable. The capture of the sensor toward H4-Au-FC and H2-Au-THI probes is close to the thermodynamic equilibrium when the incubation time is more than 60 min. The number of H4-Au-FC and H2-Au-THI probes on the electrode surface remains unchanged with the increase of incubation time. Thus, ΔIp(FC) and ΔIp(THI) values no longer substantially change with time. To obtain high sensitivity and save time, the incubation time of 60 min was used for the electrochemical detection of OME and ACE.

Fig. S2 shows the relationship curves of ΔIp(FC) and ΔIp(THI) with the volume of H4-Au-FC and H2-Au-THI. When the volume is < 8 μL, ΔIp(FC) and Ip(THI) rapidly increase with the increased volume of H4-Au-FC and H2-Au-THI. This increase is because the number of H4-Au-FC and H2-Au-THI probes in the system cannot meet the needs of DNA cycle I and II during the incubation. Under the condition, the number of redox probes immobilized on the electrode surface depends on the volume of H4-Au-FC and H2-Au-THI probes. Thus, ΔIp(FC) and ΔIp(THI) values increase with the probe concentration because of the redox of more probes on the electrode surface. However, ΔIp(FC) and ΔIp(THI) values tend to be stable when the probe volume is > 8 μL, because the number of probes at the probe concentration meet the needs of DNA cycle I and II during the incubation time. Thus, continuing to increase the probe volume does not bring a considerable increase in ΔIp(FC) and ΔIp(THI). To obtain high sensitivity and save probes, 10 μL of the H4-Au-FC solution and H2-Au-THI solution were used for the electrochemical detection of OME and ACE.

The proposed sensor was incubated at 37 °C for 60 min in the presence of OME and ACE with different concentrations to test the electrochemical detection. DPV curves were measured in the PBS of pH 7. To obtain higher sensitivity, two potential ranges, 0.05–0.45 V and − 0.45–− 0.15 V, were selected for DPV detection of ACE and OME, respectively. Fig. 8 A and C show that the DPV currents caused by OME and ACE rapidly increase with increasing OME and ACE concentrations. A higher concentration of OME or ACE releases more AS-A or AS-O from the duplex DNAs and a faster DNA cycle I or II process results with More free AS-P or AS-O. As a result, more H2-Au-THI or H4-Au-FC probes were brought to the electrode surface during the incubation of 60 min. Thus, the DPV current response will increase with OME and ACE concentration. Further, Fig. 8B and D indicate the relationship curves of DPV peak currents caused by OME and ACE with the logarithm values of OME and ACE concentrations. As shown in Fig. 8B, there is a good linear relationship between the Ip(FC) value and logarithm of OME concentration in the range of 5 × 10−14 – 5 × 10−10 M. The corresponding linear regression equation is: the Ip (FC) (nA) = 833.72 × LOG[COME, M] + 11,608, with the correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.991. Based on a signal-to-noise ratio of 3, the limit of detection (LOD) was measured and calculated. The result indicates the LOD is 1.67 × 10−14 Μ for OME. As shown in Fig. 8 D, there is a good linear relationship between the Ip(THI) value and the logarithm of ACE concentration in the range of 1 × 10−14 − 5 × 10−10 M. The linear regression equation is: Ip(FC)(nA)= −255.38 × LOG[CACE, M] − 5426.7, with the correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.998. The LOD is found to be 3.33 × 10−15 M (S/N = 3) for ACE. For comparison, analytical parameters of different methods for the detection of OME and ACE are shown in Table 1 and Table S1. Table 1 and Table S1 indicate that the proposed method has better sensitivity than the previously reported methods in the literature. It is suggested that high sensitivity is attributed to the excellent catalytic activity of Arg/Asp-GQD-Au and significant signal amplification from double target-induced DNA cycles.

Twenty modified electrodes were prepared by the same procedure and materials to test the reproducibility of electrode batch production. Their DPV peak current responses toward 1 × 10−11 M of OME (ΔIp(FC)) and 1 × 10−11 M of ACE (ΔIp(THI)) were measured, respectively. The result shows the relative standard deviation (RSD) values of detection signal among the modified electrodes are 1.7% for OME and 3.1% for ACE, indicating good reproducibility. Storage stability of the modified electrode was examined by storing the modified electrode in a refrigerator at 4 °C. Then, it was taken out every week and its DPV peak current response was measured toward 1 × 10−11 M of OME and 1 × 10−11 M of ACE. The result shows that one four-week storage only causes the change of 2.4% in DPV peak current response toward OME and 2.2% in DPV peak current response toward ACE, verifying good storage stability. The modified electrode can be reused several times because the number of H1 fixed on the electrode surface can meet the needs of many detections. The changes in DPV peak currents (ΔIp(FC) and ΔIp(THI)) caused by each detection can be used for quantification of OME and ACE. One modified electrode was employed to test the reuse for continuous detections of 1 × 10−11 M of OME and ACE. Fig. S3 shows that ΔIp(FC) and ΔIp(THI) values can remain stable when the number of detection times is < 16. However, increasing the number of detection times will cause a substantialsignal reduction. Thus, the modified electrode can be reused 16 times for the electrochemical detection of OME and ACE.

The effect of other pesticides commonly used in agricultural production on the DPV peak current response was studied. Fig. 9 A shows that only OME can produce a sensitive DPV current response, indicating a good specificity for the detection of OME. OME can hybridize with the AP-O/AS-O duplex DNA to release free AS-O. The released AS-O triggers the DNA cycle II. One OME molecule can bring several H4-Au-FC probes to the electrode surface by the DNA cycle II. The redox of FC molecules in these H4-Au-FC probes produces a sensitive DPV current response, indicating a high ΔIp(FC) value. However, imidacloprid, cypermethrin, fenvalerate, chlorothalonil, chlorfenapyr, fipronil, bifenthrin, difenoconazole,and ACE cannot hybridize with AP-O/AS-O duplex DNA to release free AS-O since no free AS-O is present in the reaction system; hence, the DNA cycle II cannot be triggered. As no H4-Au-FC probe was brought to the electrode surface, ΔIp(FC) value is close to the that of the blank sample. Fig. 9B indicates only ACE can produce high ΔIp(THI), indicating a good specificity for detection of ACE. Only ACE can hybridize with the AP-A/AS-A duplex to release free AS-A. The released AS-A triggers the DNA cycle I and leads to a sensitive DPV current response. Therefore, the proposed sensor can be directly used in the electrochemical detection of OME and ACE in vegetables and fruits.

The proposed analytical method and GC-MS [8] have been used to detect OME and ACE in vegetables and fruits samples. In addition, the recovery experiment was also carried out by adding a certain amount of OME and ACE in the samples. Table 2 shows that the results obtained by using the proposed sensor are consistent with the results obtained by GC-MS. Furthermore, the recoveries are in the range of 95.0–104.5%, indicating good precision and accuracy.

This study demonstrated the synthesis of Au nanoparticle-aspartic acid-functionalized graphene quantum dot hybrid. Aspartic acid-functionalized graphene quantum dot acts as the reducing agent, stabilizer, polydentate linker, and N-type semiconductor in the synthesis. The as-synthesized hybrid shows a pomegranate-like three-dimensional structure composed of small Au nanoparticles with a size of only several nanometers. The intimate contact of Au nanoparticles and small graphene sheets leads to the formation of Schottky heterojunction at the interface. The unique structure realizes high catalytic activity, structural stability, and electron/ion conductivity. The electrochemical sensor based on hybrid and double target-induced DNA cycles exhibits high sensitivity and selectivity for the detection of OME and ACE. Furthermore, this study provides a way for fabrication of metal–graphene quantum dot hybrids with high catalytic activity and structural stability in biosensors and catalysis.
Li Ruiyi: Investigation, Writing. Wang Jin: Investigation, Writing – review & editing. Li Nana: Investigation. Xu Dan: Investigation. Li Zaijun: Conceptualization, Methodology.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the financial support from The National Key Research and Development Program of China (No. 2018YFC1603001) and National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21576115). In addition, We also would like to acknowledge the work of the Central Laboratory, School of Chemical and Material Engineering, Jiangnan University.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.snb.2022.131597.

ce:section.ce:para:

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Li Ruiyi is one associate professor in School of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Jiangnan University, China. She received his master degree from The Birmingham University, The United Kingdom, 2014. She received his doctor degree from Jiangnan University, China, 2018. Her research interest focus on synthesis of nanomaterials and their application in analytical chemistry.

Wang Jin is currently Master candidate in School of Chemical and Material Engineering, Jiangnan University, China. Her research interest is synthesis of nanomaterials and their application in electrochemical sensors.

Li Nana is currently a PhD candidate in School of Chemical and Materials Engineering, Jiangnan University, China. Her research interest is synthesis of functionalized graphene quantum dots and their application in analytical chemistry.

Xu Dan is currently a PhD candidate in School of Chemical and Materials Engineering, Jiangnan University, China. Her research interest is synthesis of graphene-based composite and their application in electrochemical sensors.

Li Zaijun is one professor of School of Chemical and Materials Engineering, Jiangnan University, China. He received his doctor degree from Jiangnan University, China, 2004. He is author of more than 300 papers and reviewer of more than 40 journals. His current research interests are synthesis of graphene-based nanomaterials and their applications in sensing, electrocatalysis and energy storage/conversion devices.
